1: hitqosheshu vaqqoshu hagoy lo nikhsaf: 'Gather together, yes, gather, O shameless nation.' The double root qashas (hitqosheshu/vaqqoshu — to gather together) is another intensification device. Lo nikhsaf (not desiring/longing, or 'without shame') — the nation that has no longing for YHWH, or the shameless nation. The call to gather is ironic: before the Day sweeps everything away, the nation should assemble for one last act of national repentance. The call to assemble before the decree comes is the prophetic grace-window: there is still time to seek YHWH.
2: beterem ledet choq kemo mots avar yom beterem lo yavo aleichem charon af YHWH: 'Before the decree takes effect — before the day passes away like chaff — before there comes upon you the burning anger of the LORD.' The triple beterem (before, before, before) creates urgency: there is still a window of opportunity, but it is closing fast. The metaphor 'like chaff' (mots avar) — the day passes in an instant, like windblown chaff; once it arrives, it cannot be outrun. The 'decree' (choq) is the fixed divine decision that will execute judgment — once issued, irreversible.
3: baqshu et YHWH kol anve ha'aretz asher mishpato po'alu baqshu tzedek baqshu anavah: 'Seek the LORD, all you humble of the land, who do his just commands; seek righteousness, seek humility.' Three imperatives (baqshu ... baqshu ... baqshu) addressed to anve ha'aretz (the humble/poor of the land, H6035 — the lowly and meek). The word anavah (H6038, humility/meekness) is the virtue Zephaniah identifies as the proper response to the Day of Wrath. This word-pair — seeking YHWH through righteousness and humility — is the OT's most direct anticipation of the Beatitudes (Matthew 5:3-6). The humble who seek YHWH may be sheltered on the Day: ulay tissateru (perhaps you will be hidden — using the root of Zephaniah's own name, tzafan).
4: ki Aza azuvah tihyeh veAshqelon lishemamah Ashdod baytzaharayim yegerashuha veEqron teiaqer: 'For Gaza shall be forsaken and Ashkelon shall become a desolation; Ashdod's people shall be driven out at noon, and Ekron shall be uprooted.' The four major Philistine cities face judgment. The Hebrew contains deliberate wordplay: Aza azuvah (Gaza will be forsaken) — city name and fate share the same root (azav = to forsake); Ekron teiaqer (Ekron will be uprooted) — paronomasia with the city name. These sound-plays tied the name of a place to its prophetic fate — a characteristic prophetic rhetorical device.
5: hoi yoshvei chevel hayam goi Keretim: 'Woe to you inhabitants of the seacoast, you nation of the Cherethites.' The Cherethites (Keretim) are the Philistines (also the name of David's elite royal guard, 2 Samuel 8:18), associated with Crete (connected to the Sea Peoples who migrated from the Aegean). The woe oracle (hoi) shifts from the remnant call of vv1-3 to explicit judgment: the coastal nations that controlled the Mediterranean trade route will be destroyed. 'The word of the LORD is against you, O Canaan, land of the Philistines' — Canaan here = the coastal commercial region.
6: vehayah chevel hayam nevot keret ro'im ugedarot tzon: 'And you, O seacoast, shall be pastures with meadows for shepherds and folds for flocks.' The transformation from trade-route coastal cities to empty pastoral land (nevot = pastures, gedarot tzon = sheepfolds) is the reversal of urban civilization. The once-busy Philistine coast — with its ports, markets, and military fortifications — becomes a quiet pastoral scene. This transformation prepares for the remnant who will inherit the land (v7: the coast shall belong to the remnant of the house of Judah).
7: vehayah chevel lish'erit beit Yehudah: 'And the seacoast shall belong to the remnant of the house of Judah.' The remnant theology (she'erit, H7611 = remnant, what remains) is introduced: after the judgment, a remnant survives and inherits dispossessed territory. The remnant inheriting Philistine land restores Israel's ancient covenantal boundaries (cf. Genesis 15:18-21; Josh 15). YHWH will visit (paqad) them and restore their fortunes (shavah shevutam = turn their captivity/fortune): the same verb used for the Day of judgment (paqad in v9) is used for the Day of restoration — divine visitation serves both purposes.
8: shamati cherpat Moav ugidufei benei Ammon asher cherefu et ami vayagdilu al gevulam: 'I have heard the taunts of Moab and the revilings of the Ammonites, how they have taunted my people and made boasts against their territory.' Both nations (descended from Lot's incestuous unions with his daughters, Genesis 19:30-38) were persistent enemies of Israel on the eastern side of the Jordan. Their sin: taunting (cherpah) YHWH's people and expanding their territory at Israel's expense. YHWH hears the mockery directed at his people as mockery directed at himself (cf. Acts 9:4: Saul, why do you persecute me?).
9: lachen chai ani neum YHWH tzvaot Elohei Yisrael ki Moav kiSdom tihyeh uvenei Ammon ka'Amorah mimshaq charrul umichreih melach ushemamah ad olam: 'Therefore, as I live, declares the LORD of hosts, the God of Israel, Moab shall become like Sodom, and the Ammonites like Gomorrah, a land possessed by nettles and salt pits and a waste forever.' The Sodom-Gomorrah comparison (kiSdom ... ka'Amorah) is the highest register of divine judgment language in the OT: total, irreversible destruction, with Sodom and Gomorrah as the paradigm (Gen 19). The divine oath formula chai ani (as I live) — YHWH swears by his own life (there is nothing greater to swear by, Hebrews 6:13) — underscores the absolute certainty of the oracle.
10: zot lahem tachat ga'onam ki cherefu vayagdilu al am YHWH tzvaot: 'This shall happen to them in return for their pride, because they taunted and boasted against the people of the LORD of hosts.' The pride-judgment pattern (ga'avah → judgment) is the prophetic axiom: pride against YHWH's people is pride against YHWH himself, and pride is the sin YHWH most systematically opposes (Proverbs 3:34: He opposes the proud but gives grace to the humble; cited in James 4:6; 1 Peter 5:5).
11: nora YHWH aleihem ki razah et kol elohei ha'aretz veyishtachavu lo ish mimqomo kol iyei hagoyim: 'The LORD will be awesome/terrible against them when he makes all the gods of the earth shrivel up, and to him shall bow down, each in its place, all the lands of the nations.' The verb razah (H7329, to make thin, waste away) applied to the gods of the nations: YHWH will starve/wither the deities of the Gentiles into nothing, demonstrating their non-existence. The result: the nations, deprived of their false gods, will bow (yishtachavu) to YHWH from their own places — a universal worship from all coastlands/islands. This eschatological vision of universal worship is the OT background for Philippians 2:10-11 (every knee shall bow at the name of Jesus).
12: vegam atem kushim challelei charbi hemah: 'You also, O Cushites, shall be slain by my sword.' The judgment oracle extends to Cush (Ethiopia/Nubia, H3568) — the most distant nation known to the ancient Near Eastern world. The inclusion of Cush (along with Philistia v4, Moab/Ammon vv8-9, and Assyria vv13-15) establishes the comprehensive geographical scope of YHWH's sovereignty: from the Mediterranean coast to Transjordan, to Africa, to the superpower of the north. No nation is beyond his reach.
13: veyeT yadyo al tzafon veyabed et Ashshur veyasem et Nineveh lishmamah tziyah kamidbar: 'And he will stretch out his hand against the north and destroy Assyria, and he will make Nineveh a desolation, a dry waste like the desert.' The stretching of YHWH's hand (yetT yadyo) is the Exodus gesture of judgment (Exodus 6:6; Zephaniah 7:5). Assyria, the dominant world power of Zephaniah's era — Nineveh boasted walls 8 miles in circumference — will be made a desert. The prediction was fulfilled in 612 BCE when Nineveh fell to the Babylonians and Medes. The prophecy in its original context was audacious: condemning the most powerful empire in the world.
14: veravetzu veqirbah adarim kol chayat goy gam qaat gam qipod vekaptoreiha yalinu: 'Herds shall lie down in her midst, all kinds of beasts; even the owl and the hedgehog shall lodge in her capitals; a voice shall hoot in the window.' The desolation portrait of Nineveh — animals inhabiting the great city — is the prophetic reversal of civilization. The capitals (kaptoreiha) of great buildings become owl-roosts; the hoot replaces the shout of merchants and warriors. The same reversal portrait appears in Isaiah 13:21-22 (Babylon's desolation) and Isaiah 34:11-15 (Edom's desolation) — the city that was great shall become wilderness habitat.
15: zot ha'ir ha'aliza hayoshevet labetach hamerot levavah ein od milevadeiy: 'This is the exultant city that lived securely, that said in her heart, I am, and there is no one else.' Nineveh's proud self-declaration — ani ve'efsi od (I am, and there is no one else) — is the language of deity applied to a human empire. YHWH uses exactly the same declaration for himself (Isaiah 45:5-6: I am YHWH, and there is no other). By claiming YHWH's exclusive divine formula, Nineveh has committed the ultimate act of idolatrous self-deification. The reversal: the city that claimed to be the only one becomes a desolation. Her taunt-verse ends the chapter — the great city's pride spoken back to her as the narration of her ruin.
